An example of the power of the principle of social proof comes from Colleen Schott, one of the most successful script writers in the infomercial industry, which creates late night TV programming, selling everything from cooking equipment to car wax to nutritional supplements. Colleen is unusual in her profession because she operates as a rigorous experimenter, changing just one feature of an ad at a time and testing the effect on sales of that single alteration. In one such case, she altered the familiar call to action line that we hear at the end of each ad from, operators are waiting, please call now, to, if operators are busy, please call again. Now, you might say, huh, telling people that they might be inconvenienced if they try to buy a product goes against all marketing logic. Well, maybe so. But it fits with the psychological logic of social proof. Think of the image produced by the standard call to action line. Operators are waiting, please call now. It's a bunch of operators sitting around, twiddling their thumbs and polishing their nails because nobody's calling. Compare that to the image produced by the line, if operators are busy, please call again. It means a lot of people just like you are already calling, which is pure social proof. The result of the change? Call skyrocket to a level that shocked everyone associated with the infomercial. You've probably heard the slogan, power to the people with social proof. It's power from the people. That's where social proof gets its force.
